questions.question: He's ___ a great new job in the city.
___ you ever ridden a horse?
They ___ been best friends since primary school.
Has he ___ the email I sent him?
She hasn't ___ her driving test yet.
Have you ___ a decision about the job offer?
I've just ___ coffee all over my laptop!
He hasn't ___ to his parents since last Christmas.
Has the last bus already ___?
I have never ___ so embarrassed in my whole life!
Have you ___ the latest news about the election?
We've already ___ our decision.
She has ___ three books this month.
Have they ever ___ abroad for more than a year?
___ you taken your medicine today?
I've ___ seen such a beautiful painting before!
She's ___ the doctor already.
They've ___ all the pizza. There's nothing left!
I ___ lived in this city since I was born.
He has ___ more than 50 countries in his lifetime.

theory.explanation:
Present Perfect tense

Form: have / has + past participle (3rd form of the verb)

When we use it:
  • Life experience (ever, never): "Have you ever been abroad?"
  • Actions just completed (just, already): "I've just finished."
  • A result visible now: "She has lost her keys." (she still doesn't have them)
  • Duration up to now (for, since): "I've lived here for ten years."

Patterns:
  Affirmative: I/You/We/They have + pp    |  He/She/It has + pp
  Negative:    haven't + pp               |  hasn't + pp
  Questions:   Have + subject + pp?     |  Has + subject + pp?

examples.sentence: He's found a great new job.
examples.explanation: Affirmative: he + has (= he's) + found (pp of 'find'). Result visible now.

examples.sentence: Have you ever ridden a horse?
examples.explanation: Question with ever: Have + you + ridden (pp of 'ride'). Ever comes after have/has.

examples.sentence: She hasn't passed her driving test yet.
examples.explanation: Negative: she + hasn't + passed + yet. Yet = not yet — always at the end.
